Q1: I tried to keep a somewhat consistent study routine, usually dedicating about three to four hours each day across the week. Since I'm a visual learner, I'd often create diagrams and mind maps after watching the course videos. I'd typically study in the evenings after my extracurricular activities, though I'll admit my routine wasn't always perfect. I aimed for around 23 hours a week, which felt manageable most of the time.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. I tried to maintain regular study times, but honestly, deadlines would push me to adjust everything. Some weeks I'd be more relaxed and spread things out, but other weeks I'd have to cram more hours in when assignments were coming up. Life has a way of disrupting even the best plans, you know?
Q3: Usually it was either checking for new announcements or seeing if there were new video lectures posted. Sometimes I'd log in because I remembered an assignment deadline was approaching and I needed to check the details. I also went in to participate in discussions when I felt I had something meaningful to contribute. It wasn't always a daily habit, but I logged in fairly regularly.
Q4: After a few weeks, checking the LMS in the evening became somewhat automatic for me. I'd finish dinner, do my extracurricular stuff, then sit down and open the platform. The mindfulness exercises I was doing actually helped me create this calm transition into study mode. It wasn't perfect consistency, but it became a recognizable pattern in my week.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me because I learn best through visual content. I watched over 250 minutes of video content, and I'd often pause to sketch out concepts or take visual notes. The discussion forums were also helpful because seeing how other students understood the material gave me different perspectives. Those two resources together really anchored my learning.
Q6: I'll be honest, I didn't complete all the readings and some of the text-heavy supplementary materials. Without good internet access at home, downloading everything was a hassle, so I'd prioritize videos over PDFs. Some optional quizzes I skipped when time was tight. I focused on what I knew would help me most given my learning style.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a clear connection to the exams or assignments. Visual content like videos and diagrams always felt valuable because that's how information sticks for me. If something seemed like busywork or didn't clearly contribute to my understanding, I'd deprioritize it. I needed to see the payoff for my effort.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—like they were bonuses rather than necessities. I'd engage with optional content when I was genuinely curious or had extra time, which wasn't always. Required activities had this weight to them that made me prioritize them even when I was tired. The optional stuff sometimes felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download videos when I could access good internet, then watch them at home where my connection is unreliable. I created physical notebooks full of diagrams and visual summaries that I'd review constantly. I also used some external YouTube videos to supplement concepts I found confusing in the course materials.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes and diagrams without touching the LMS at all. I'd sit with my visual summaries, testing myself and redrawing concepts from memory. Sometimes I'd study with classmates in person, working through problems together on a whiteboard. None of that showed up anywhere in the system.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my main approach because of my internet situation. I'd access a video once, download it if possible, or take detailed visual notes during that single viewing. Then I'd return to those notes repeatedly over the following days and weeks. The LMS would only show that one access, but I'd have studied that content many times over.
Q12: There were definitely harder moments, usually when multiple deadlines converged or when I felt overwhelmed by the volume of content. My attendance dropped to around 78% partly because of these overwhelming periods. The mindfulness practice helped me push through, but sometimes I just needed to step back and breathe before re-engaging. Those dips were temporary though.
Q13: Competing responsibilities definitely affected my LMS usage. Between extracurricular activities and life stuff, sometimes I just couldn't log in as often as I wanted. Fatigue was real too—after a long day, staring at a screen wasn't always appealing. I'd compensate by doing more offline studying with my notes when screen time felt exhausting.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd be watching a video but my mind would wander completely. I'd technically be logged in and the video would be playing, but I wasn't really absorbing anything. Those moments usually happened when I was tired or stressed about other things. I learned to recognize when I was just going through the motions and needed a real break.
Q15: I definitely postponed some assignments even when I knew they mattered—my completion rate of about 53% reflects that honestly. Sometimes the task felt too big to start, or I'd convince myself I'd do it better tomorrow. Other times I was genuinely busy with extracurriculars or dealing with internet access issues. Procrastination was a real challenge I had to keep fighting.
Q16: Deadlines were huge motivators for me. I'd often see a spike in my activity right before something was due. They gave structure to what could otherwise feel like an overwhelming amount of content. Without deadlines, I might have drifted more, so even though they caused stress, they kept me accountable and moving forward.
Q17: When I got feedback on assignments, it definitely influenced how I approached the next ones. Positive feedback motivated me to keep going with similar strategies. Constructive criticism helped me adjust my visual note-taking and focus areas. I appreciated feedback that was specific because it gave me clear direction for improvement.
Q18: The course structure with its 20 modules was both helpful and challenging. Having clear modules helped me organize my study plan and know what to tackle next. But the sheer volume sometimes felt overwhelming, especially when I fell behind. I appreciated when modules built on each other logically because it helped me see the bigger picture.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier for someone like me who learns visually. However, the heavy reliance on consistent internet access made things harder given my situation at home. Discussion forums were good but sometimes felt like extra work on top of everything else. A more flexible approach to offline access would have helped me a lot.
Q20: I think students might be exploring, trying to understand what's expected, or struggling to figure out how to actually complete the work. Sometimes you can spend hours watching videos and reading without producing anything tangible. There's also the possibility of personal issues or confusion about assignments that creates a gap between activity and output.
Q21: That actually resonates with my experience somewhat. I performed reasonably well on exams despite not having the highest LMS engagement because I studied extensively offline. My visual notes and diagrams were my real study tools, not the platform itself. The LMS was just a starting point for content I'd then work with independently.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you log in and how long videos play, which gives a surface-level picture. But they miss the depth of engagement—whether someone is actually thinking critically or just passively watching. They definitely miss all offline studying, peer discussions, and the mental effort that happens away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material and trying to understand it deeply, not just completing tasks. For me, it's when I'm actively creating visual representations and connecting new concepts to what I already know. It's feeling curious about the content and motivated to master it. True engagement happens in my mind, not just on a platform.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much time I spent with my handwritten visual notes or how often I redrew diagrams from memory. It wouldn't capture the mindfulness practice that helped me stay emotionally balanced and approach studying with a clearer head. It also wouldn't show the conversations with classmates or the genuine happiness I felt when concepts finally clicked for me.
